metrics.description: The average number of instructions retired per CPU Cycle. The lower IPC value means the application consumes more cycles due to cache misses, branch mispredictions, pipeline stalls, I/O or any other performance bottlenecks.
metrics.name: IPC (Sys + User)
metrics.title: IPC (Sys + User)

metrics.description: The average number of instructions retired per CPU Cycle in kernel space. The lower IPC value means the application consumes more cycles due to cache misses, branch mispredictions, pipeline stalls, I/O or any other performance bottlenecks.
metrics.name: IPC (Sys)
metrics.title: IPC (Sys)

metrics.description: The average number of instructions retired per CPU Cycle in user space. The lower IPC value means the application consumes more cycles due to cache misses, branch mispredictions, pipeline stalls, I/O or any other performance bottlenecks.
metrics.name: IPC (User)
metrics.title: IPC (User)

metrics.description: Cycles per instruction retired; indicating how much time each executed instruction took, in units of cycles. Often this metric shows how efficiently applications are using the underlying hardware. A lower CPI could indicate that instructions are not hitting bottlenecks and retiring quickly.
metrics.name: CPI (Sys + User)
metrics.title: CPI (Sys + User)

description: The Front-end is responsible for fetching the program code represented in architectural instructions and decoding them into one or more low-level hardware operations called micro-ops (uOps).
Usually, most uOps pass completely through the pipeline and retire, but sometimes speculatively fetched uOps may get cancelled before retirement - like in the case of mis-predicted branches.
The Back-end is responsible for monitoring when uOps data operands are available and executing the uOp in an available execution unit.
Retirement is where results of the uOp are committed to the architectural state (CPU registers or written back to memory).
subsections.name: Pipeline Utilization Analysis

subsections.description: Percentage of time spent in the active CPU power state C0. When a core is not being utilized it can go into lower power states to reduce idle energy consumption.
subsections.name: CPU Utilization

subsections.description: This is the speed of the CPU clock. Higher frequencies allow faster execution. Usually servers are able to change their frequency based on utilization to conserve energy or turbo for short periods of time to boost performance.
subsections.name: CPU Average Frequency (MHz)

description: Cycles per instruction retired; indicating how much time each executed instruction took; in units of cycles. Often this metric shows how efficiently applications are using the underlying hardware. A lower CPI could indicate that instructions are not hitting bottlenecks and retiring quickly.

The average number of instructions retired per CPU Cycle. The lower IPC value means the application consumes more cycles due to cache misses, branch mispredictions, pipeline stalls, I/O or any other performance bottlenecks.
